entry.title: Ranting About Group Projects
entry.content:
Group projects are just wonderful

Two people playing dead[^playdead], haven’t messaged me once since the deadline

Two more, after I poured enormous effort into guiding and communicating, just fed me AI-generated garbage

One genius in particular even rearranged the original text’s order, probably figured Hugo’s literary skills weren’t quite up to par while copying

I set a new personal best of 0.000001 seconds in the not-tilting[^tilting] challenge, you should try it too

When the group project started, to maintain cohesion and with the competition coming up, I drafted an outline myself

Then spent two evening self-study[^selfstudy] sessions on background reading and completely restructured the whole outline

Made documents about work principles and division of labor, the Virginia incident, excerpts from Les Misrables, all materials distributed and ready

If the documents weren’t clear enough, coming to ask was fine, but after teaching people like middle schoolers what summarizing means, what the difference is between facts and arguments, after all that communication cost, what I get back is logically flawed, content-vague AI slop

Even though I clearly spelled out in the documents and conversations to avoid exactly this, some people still don’t get it. The communication cost alone would’ve been enough for me to finish everything myself, without having to send it back for revisions

At this point, why did this even happen?

In the end, joining the group means happily becoming the leader, no matter how much you put in, there’s no return. It’s only natural to become a beast of burden[^beast], feeling excluded, how is this not a form of bullying

** Comments**

  • LoulanRE (2024-11-03 00:25): Warning everyone, either find responsible members, or make the lazy dogs buy indulgences[^indulgences] to escape labor. There is no third path.
  • Ji reply @LoulanRE (2024-11-03 00:58): I’m the one who voluntarily buys indulgences, mainly covering plagiarism check fees
  • LoulanRE reply @Ji (2024-11-03 01:06): Good kid, come join the group, you’ve done great already
  • Ji reply @LoulanRE (2024-11-03 01:07): Turns out our school doesn’t even have requirements for plagiarism check websites, Xuexitong[^xuexitong] welcomes him. Wanted to buy indulgences but couldn’t spend the money
  • Ji reply @LoulanRE (2024-11-03 01:07): Actually this also depends on the team’s strength, if plagiarism rates are always too high it’s ruined
  • optional (2024-11-03 00:47): How can they be so disrespectful of others’ effort…
  • LoulanRE reply @optional (2024-11-03 01:07):
  • Anonymous (2024-11-03 01:16): Poor Weipeng
  • Qi reply @Anonymous (2024-11-03 01:36): Weipeng?
  • Zhang (2024-11-03 09:45): What a legend

[^playdead]: “Playing dead”, literally “lying like a corpse”?is Chinese internet slang for doing absolutely nothing, vanishing without contributing. Much stronger than “slacking,”it implies complete non-existence in group work.
[^tilting]: “Tilting”(㈡), literally “red-temperature”?is Chinese gaming slang for becoming so furious your face turns red and heats up. Equivalent to being “tilted”or “losing your cool”in English gaming culture.
[^selfstudy]: Evening self-study ( is a mandatory supervised study period common in Chinese universities and high schools, typically running from 7, 0 PM. Attendance may be tracked.
[^beast]: “Beast of burden”(┈), literally “oxen and horses”?is Chinese internet slang for someone who is exploited and overworked, akin to “wage slave”or “drudge.”
[^indulgences]: Indulgences (, here used humorously, borrowing the Catholic concept of selling indulgences to atone for sins. In the group project context, it means paying money to opt out of labor.
[^xuexitong]: Xuexitong (︿, is a widely used Chinese online learning platform, often associated with its plagiarism detection system that many universities require for submitting assignments.

entry.title: 'Uma Musume: Road to Top Promo'
entry.content:
The hunger for the path to victory, who among us does not share it?
The Triple Crown[^1] dream blooms, the Triple Crown dream shatters
But the goddess of victory forever smiles upon only one
One horse charges ahead, ten thousand horses fade to shadow
How do you answer expectations, How do you honor the hardship?
The path of atonement, the fulfillment of promises, the dawn of a conqueror
Remorse and unyielding spirit, how does one grow under the pounding of reality
A story that points straight to the pinnacle
Also, with Cygames[^2] themselves in the ring, the visual quality is seriously amazing
Lines, storyboarding, lighting and shadow, that sense of power and speed

** Comments**

  • abc (2024-11-03 01:47): Great watch

[^1]: Triple Crown, in Japanese horse racing, the Triple Crown consists of the Satsuki Sho (2000 Guineas), the Japanese Derby (Tokyo Yushun), and the Kikuka Sho (St. Leger). Winning all three in a single year is one of the rarest and most prestigious achievements in the sport.
[^2]: Cygames (cy), a Japanese video game developer and publisher, the creator of Uma Musume: Pretty Derby. “”(getting personally involved) refers to Cygames directly producing the anime, resulting in notably high production quality.

entry.title: Introduction - Preface
entry.content:
Epigraph

Who can unravel the essence, the stamp of the artistic temperament! Who can grasp the deep, instinctual fusion of discipline and dissipation on which it rests! HOMAS MANN, Death in Venice

Original Text

Nearly every weekday morning for a year and a half, I got up at 5:30, brushed my teeth, made a cup of coffee, and sat down to write about how some of the greatest minds of the past four hundred years approached this exact same taskhat is, how they made the time each day to do their best work, how they organized their schedules in order to be creative and productive. By writing about the admittedly mundane details of my subjects’ daily liveshen they slept and ate and worked and worried hoped to provide a novel angle on their personalities and careers, to sketch entertaining, small-bore portraits of the artist as a creature of habit.

“Tell me what you eat, and I shall tell you what you are,”the French gastronome Jean Anthelme Brillat-Savarin once wrote. I say, tell me what time you eat, and whether you take a nap afterward.

In that sense, this is a superficial book. It’s about the circumstances of creative activity, not the product; it deals with manufacturing rather than meaning. But it’s also, inevitably, personal. (John Cheever thought that you couldn’t even type a business letter without revealing something of your inner selfsn’t that the truth?) My underlying concerns in the book are issues that I struggle with in my own life: How do you do meaningful creative work while also earning a living, Is it better to devote yourself wholly to a project or to set aside a small portion of each day, And when there doesn’t seem to be enough time for all you hope to accomplish, must you give things up (sleep, income, a clean house), or can you learn to condense activities, to do more in less time, to “work smarter, not harder,”as my dad is always telling me, More broadly, are comfort and creativity incompatible, or is the opposite true: Is finding a basic level of daily comfort a prerequisite for sustained creative work?

I don’t pretend to answer these questions in the following pagesrobably some of them can’t be answered, or can be resolved only individually, in shaky personal compromisesut I have tried to provide examples of how a variety of brilliant and successful people have confronted many of the same challenges. I wanted to show how grand creative visions translate to small daily increments; how one’s working habits influence the work itself, and vice versa.

It was a familiar predicament. I’m a classic “morning person,”capable of considerable focus in the early hours but pretty much useless after lunch.

That afternoon, to make myself feel better about this often inconvenient predilection (who wants to get up at 5:30 every day?), I started searching the Internet for information about other writers’ working schedules.

These were easy to find, and highly entertaining. It occurred to me that someone should collect these anecdotes in one placeence the Daily Routines blog I launched that very afternoon (my magazine story got written in a last-minute panic the next morning) and, now, this book.

The blog was a casual affair; I merely posted descriptions of people’s routines as I ran across them in biographies, magazine profiles, newspaper obits, and the like.

For the book, I’ve pulled together a vastly expanded and better-researched collection, while also trying to maintain the brevity and diversity of voices that made the original appealing.

As much as possible, I’ve let my subjects speak for themselves, in quotes from letters, diaries, and interviews. In other cases, I have cobbled together a summary of their routines from secondary sources.

And when another writer has produced the perfect distillation of his subject’s routine, I have quoted it at length rather than try to recast it myself.

I should note here that this book would have been impossible without the research and writing of the hundreds of biographers, journalists, and scholars whose work I drew upon.

I have documented all of my sources in the Notes section, which I hope will also serve as a guide to further reading.

Compiling these entries, I kept in mind a passage from a 1941 essay by V. S. Pritchett.

Writing about Edward Gibbon, Pritchett takes note of the great English historian’s remarkable industryven during his military service, Gibbon managed to find the time to continue his scholarly work, toting along Horace on the march and reading up on pagan and Christian theology in his tent.

“Sooner or later,”Pritchett writes, “the great men turn out to be all alike. They never stop working. They never lose a minute. It is very depressing.”

What aspiring writer or artist has not felt this exact sentiment from time to time?

Looking at the achievements of past greats is alternately inspiring and utterly discouraging.

But Pritchett is also, of course, wrong. For every cheerfully industrious Gibbon who worked nonstop and seemed free of the self-doubt and crises of confidence that dog us mere mortals, there is a William James or a Franz Kafka, great minds who wasted time, waited vainly for inspiration to strike, experienced torturous blocks and dry spells, were racked by doubt and insecurity.

In reality, most of the people in this book are somewhere in the middleommitted to daily work but never entirely confident of their progress; always wary of the one-off day that undoes the streak.

All of them made the time to get their work done. But there is infinite variation in how they structured their lives to do so.

This book is about that variation. And I hope that readers will find it encouraging rather than depressing.

Writing it, I often thought of a line from a letter Kafka sent to his beloved Felice Bauer in 1912.

Frustrated by his cramped living situation and his deadening day job, he complained, “time is short, my strength is limited, the office is a horror, the apartment is noisy, and if a pleasant, straightforward life is not possible then one must try to wriggle through by subtle maneuvers.”

Poor Kafka! But then who among us can expect to live a pleasant, straightforward life?

For most of us, much of the time, it is a slog, and Kafka’s subtle maneuvers are not so much a last resort as an ideal.

Here’s to wriggling through.

Vocabulary Beyond 3500 Most Common Words / Idioms / Words with Uncommon Meanings

Although I have marked the vocabulary here, it is quite inconvenient to look them up while reading. I am used to reading these on a tablet before bed, so for mobile users I recommend Eudic[^eudic], which supports direct selection and lookupuick and lightweight. On PC, I use Saladict[^saladict], which can display English definitions directly.

unravel [nrvl] - to untangle; to clarify

stamp [stmp] - a distinctive mark or trait (uncommon meaning of a common word; common meaning: “postage stamp; to step heavily”)

instinctual [nstktul] - instinctive, intuitive

dissipation [dspen] - indulgence, dissolute living (uncommon meaning; common meaning: “dispersal, dissipation of energy”; here meaning the opposite of self-discipline)

mundane [mnden] - ordinary, dull admittedly [dmtdli] - it must be admitted, by common consent

novel [nvl] - new and original (uncommon meaning of a common word; common meaning: “a fictional book”)

sketch [sket] - to outline briefly (uncommon meaning of a common word; common meaning: “a quick drawing”)

gastronome [strnm] - a connoisseur of good food; epicure

superficial [supfl] - shallow, on the surface

underlying [ndla] - fundamental, latent

condense [kndens] - to compress, to abridge (uncommon meaning of a common word; common meaning: “to change from gas to liquid”)

incompatible [nkmptbl] - unable to coexist, contradictory

prerequisite [prirekwzt] - a necessary condition, prerequisite

portraits [ptrts] - painted or drawn likenesses of a person

small-bore [sml b(r)] - unimportant, trivial

approach [prt] - to deal with, to tackle (uncommon meaning of a common word; common meaning: “to come near”)

manufacturing [mnjufktr] - the process of making, production (here showing how a shift in part of speech changes the nuance; common meaning: verb “to manufacture”)

individually [ndvduli] - separately, one by one

shaky [eki] - unstable, unreliable

compromise [kmprmaz] - a settlement of differences by mutual concession

confront [knfrnt] - to face, to deal with

translate [trnslet] - to transform, to convert (uncommon meaning; common meaning: “to render from one language to another”)

increment [krmnt] - a small increase, addition

vice versa [vas vs] - Latin phrase meaning “the other way around”

connote [knt] - to imply, to suggest beyond literal meaning

calibrated [klbretd] - carefully adjusted, precisely set

foster [fst(r)] - to promote, to cultivate

groove [ruv] - a settled routine, a habit (uncommon meaning; common meaning: “a long narrow channel”)

stave off [stev f] - to ward off, to hold at bay

tyranny [trni] - oppressive power, authoritarian control

chronic [krnk] - long-lasting, persistent

procrastinator [prkrstnet(r)] - someone who habitually delays or puts things off

inspired [nspad] - stimulated to creative action, having a flash of brilliance

bout [bat] - a short period of intense activity

compulsively [kmplsvli] - irresistibly, in an uncontrollable manner

tidying [tad] - putting in order, cleaning up

cubicle [kjubkl] - a small partitioned workspace

kitchenette [ktnet] - a small cooking area, mini-kitchen

predicament [prdkmnt] - a difficult, unpleasant, or embarrassing situation

predilection [pridlekn] - a strong liking, a preference

anecdote [nkdt] - a short amusing or interesting story

autopilot [tpalt] - automatic pilot; (figuratively) acting in a mechanical, unconscious state

buckling down - to start working seriously, to apply oneself fully (verb phrase)

profile [prfal] - a brief biographical sketch, an overview

obit [bt] - an obituary (informal)

brevity [brevti] - conciseness, briefness

cobble together - to assemble hastily or improvisationally

distillation [dstlen] - the process of extracting the essential meaning (often related to condensing something to its purest form)

recast [rikst] - to rewrite, to present in a different form

document [dkjumnt] - to record (verb; uncommon meaning, the common meaning is the noun “a written record”)

compile [kmpal] - to assemble, to put together from various sources

entry [entri] - an item in a collection or list

industry [ndstri] - diligence, hard work (uncommon meaning; common meaning: “a sector of the economy”)

tote [tt] - to carry

read up on - to study thoroughly, to research

pagan [pen] - relating to non-Christian or pre-Christian religions

theology [ili] - the study of the divine and religious belief

aspiring [spar] - ambitious, hoping to achieve something

alternately [ltntli] - by turns, in rotation

industrious [ndstris] - diligent, hardworking

nonstop [nnstp] - without stopping, continuously

dog [d] - to hound, to plague persistently (uncommon meaning of a common word; common meaning: the animal)

mortal [mtl] - a human being (as opposed to a god)

vainly [venli] - fruitlessly, in vain

torturous [ttrs] - agonizing, excruciating

dry spell [dra spel] - a period of creative drought, a barren stretch

rack [rk] - to torment, to cause extreme suffering

wary [weri] - cautious, watchful

undo [ndu] - to ruin, to reverse

streak [strik] - an unbroken series, a run

cramped [krmpt] - uncomfortably confined, cramped

deadening [dedn] - numbing, stupefyingly dull

wriggle [rl] - to twist and turn; to maneuver out of a tight spot

maneuver [mnuv(r)] - a strategic move; a deliberate action

slog [sl] - to work hard and steadily through something difficult

last resort [lst rzt] - the final option when all else has failed

Cultural Concepts Supplement

Thomas Mann was a renowned German writer. Born into a prominent family in Germany, his early experiences were rich and varied. His works blend realism, symbolism, and psychoanalysis, excelling at probing the inner worlds of his characters. Notable works include Buddenbrooks and The Magic Mountain. Initially influenced by nationalism, he later became a republican, and his works were banned in Germany due to their political leanings. He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1929 and remains a pivotal figure in 20th-century German literature, holding an eminent position in the literary world.

Death in Venice is Thomas Mann’s classic novella, telling the story of writer Aschenbach who, while vacationing in Venice, develops complex and intense feelings for a youth named Tadzio. Caught in the conflict between desire and reason, he persists in pursuing these feelings despite a cholera outbreak, ultimately leading to his death. The work profoundly explores themes of art, beauty, desire, morality, and life, depicting human struggle and complexity with delicate prose. It carries profound literary value and intellectual depth, stands as one of his representative works, and occupies an important place in world literary history.

Nespresso is a well-known coffee brand under Nestl, founded in Switzerland in 1986, renowned for its innovative capsule coffee system. It sells or licenses various coffee machines and a wide range of coffee capsule flavors, offering convenient operation and consistent quality (this is not an adefore looking it up, I thought it was a type of coffee like latte or espresso).

Edward Gibbon (May 8, 1737, January 16, 1794) was a distinguished English historian, politician, and essayist. Born into a wealthy family in Putney, near London, he was plagued by illness from childhood and raised by his aunt. His magnum opus, The History of the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire, published between 1776 and 1788, is celebrated for its elegant prose, rich historical sources, and incisive commentary. It is a profoundly influential historical work that established his lofty standing in historiography, and he is hailed as the father of English Enlightenment historiography.

V. S. Pritchett (December 16, 1900, March 20, 1997) was a celebrated English writer, literary critic, and screenwriter. Best known for his short stories, his works include The Spanish Virgin and Other Stories and others; his fiction often depicts middle-class life with a sharp, satirical style. He also authored memoirs such as A Cab at the Door and Midnight Oil, along with numerous literary biographies and essay collections. Pritchett wrote for The Christian Science Monitor and The New Statesman, later serving as literary editor of The New Statesman. He was knighted for his contributions to literature and received multiple awards including the Heinemann Award and the PEN Award.

Original author: Mason Currey, a Los Angelesased writer

[^eudic]: Eudic (ц) is a popular Chinese-English dictionary app known for its lightweight design and seamless in-app lookup, a favorite among Chinese students reading English texts on mobile.
[^saladict]: Saladict is a browser extension for desktop that provides instant pop-up dictionary definitions on hover, widely used by Chinese learners for reading English web content.

entry.title: The Last Veteran of the International Brigades Has Passed
entry.content:
On May 24, 2021, International Brigades veteran Josep Eduardo Almudéver Mateu passed away. With his death, every last soldier of this great army that fought for ideals and convictions has now faded into history.

Throughout the Spanish Civil War, seven International Brigades were formed in succession.

The first International Brigade was established in October 1936, arriving in Albacete, Spain on the 14th, and reaching the Madrid front on November 8.

On November 8, 1936, the streets of Madrid rang with The Internationale sung in Chinese, French, English, German, and more — the first International Brigade had arrived in Madrid to support the Spanish people’s struggle against fascism.

They were anti-fascists from 54 nations. Risking persecution by their own reactionary governments, evading layer upon layer of military police and secret agents, they trekked across mountains and rivers to reach Spain.

The last brigade was formed at the end of 1937. Over that period, more than 35,000 volunteers served in the International Brigades, and over 5,000 gave their lives.

Among them were writers and artists like Hemingway, Picasso, Orwell, Neruda, Camus, Saint-Exupéry, and Sartre — people from all over the world who cherished freedom and justice, carrying the noblest spirit of internationalism, journeying to foreign lands, marching to the front lines for the freedom of humanity, shedding their blood for the liberation of a people not their own.

“I have come here voluntarily, to save freedom in Spain and throughout the world, and if necessary I will give my last drop of blood.”

The Communists differ from other proletarian parties only in this: on the one hand, in the struggles of the proletarians of different nations, they stress and uphold the common interests of the entire proletariat, independent of nationality; on the other hand, in the various stages of development through which the struggle between the proletariat and the bourgeoisie passes, they always represent the interests of the movement as a whole. — The Communist Manifesto

(The two songs below: one is Forward, International Brigades, and the other is the elegy sung when the last veteran of the Abraham Lincoln Battalion passed away in 2016.)

entry.title: Lenin's 150th Birthday
entry.content:
To the forever great revolutionary teacher, Lenin’s 150th birthday! The red flag shall continue to fly, The Internationale shall ultimately resound across the earth, and every thunderclap shall be a salute to the revolutionaries of the old world!

“I want people to know that in that pathetic era of idealism, a group of Communist Youth League members came to the depths of the Siberian forests, chasing a phantom there, and devoted their entire lives to it.”?Liu Cixin, Ball Lightning

** Comments**

  • Long (2020-04-22 07:13): Salute!!!

entry.title: Mermaid Data Visualization Demo
entry.content:
Blog Data Visualization: The Complete Guide to Mermaid Diagrams

This article demonstrates how to use Mermaid in your blog to create elegant data visualizations. All diagrams are written directly in Markdown code blocks, no external services needed.

Tech Stack & Plugins

Layer Tech/Plugin Description
Blog Framework Hexov8.1.1 Static blog generator
Theme Butterflyv5.5.4 Built-in Mermaid support, configure mermaid.code_write: true
Diagram Engine Mermaid JavaScript-based diagramming tool, supports flowcharts, sequence diagrams, Gantt charts, etc.
Syntax Conversion mermaid-convert.js Custom Hexo Filter plugin, automatically converts ````mermaidcode blocks to Butterfly's{% mermaid %}` tag
Style Theme darkmode.css Custom dark theme variables, ensures diagrams are clearly readable in dark mode

1. Flowchart

Flowcharts are the most commonly used diagram type, perfect for illustrating workflows, decision processes, and more.

Use Cases

  • Workflow documentation
  • Decision trees
  • Algorithm logic
  • System architecture

2. Sequence Diagram

Sequence diagrams show the order of interactions between objects, particularly suited for describing API calls, user actions, and similar scenarios.

Use Cases

  • Data statistics
  • Proportion analysis
  • Time allocation
  • Category display

4. Gantt Chart

Gantt charts are a project management staple, clearly showing task timelines.

Use Cases

  • Project progress management
  • Study plans
  • Release roadmaps
  • Task dependency relationships

5. Class Diagram

Class diagrams are used to illustrate code structure and object-oriented design.

Use Cases

  • Code documentation
  • Database design
  • API structure descriptions
  • System architecture

6. State Diagram

State diagrams show state changes and transition conditions of objects.

Use Cases

  • Process state management
  • Lifecycle descriptions
  • Business rules
  • State machine design

7. ER Diagram (Entity Relationship)

ER diagrams illustrate relationships between database entities.

Use Cases

  • Database design
  • Data model descriptions
  • API relationship documentation
  • System architecture

8. Mindmap

Mindmaps are great for presenting hierarchical knowledge structures.

Use Cases

  • Knowledge organization
  • Idea structuring
  • Project planning
  • Learning paths

Advanced Tips

Custom Styles

Mermaid supports custom colors and styles:

Subgraphs

Use subgraphs to organize complex diagrams:

Usage Tips

  1. Keep it simple: Each diagram should convey one core idea
  2. Choose wisely: Select the appropriate diagram type based on your data
  3. Mind readability: Avoid overly complex nested structures
  4. Add explanations: Pair diagrams with text descriptions
  5. Test rendering: Preview before publishing to ensure proper display

Related Resources

  • Mermaid Official Documentation
  • Mermaid Live Editor
  • Butterfly Theme Documentation

Tip: All diagrams in this article are real-time rendered SVGs , they support zooming and printing, and require no external image resources.

entry.title: Midterm Exam Pain Biweek
entry.content:
Midterm Pain Biweek

God, are you kidding me, Isn’t my first-level discipline[^1] computer science, Why am I in the Automation College studying Signals and Systems, It’s so damn hard

Learning Rust that my own department doesn’t teach, Signals and Systems that the CS College doesn’t teach, Machine Learning that the Communications department doesn’t teach — the hilarious curriculum[^2] of a hybrid’s identity anxiety and crisis of belonging. At least I laughed myself angry

But I’m a creep, I’m a weirdo
What the hell am I doing here?
What the hell am I doing here,
I don’t belong here.
I don’t belong here



💬 Comments

  • Wuxing (2025-11-30 00:59): The ultimate polymath of engineering
  • LoulanRE reply @Wuxing (2025-11-30 01:12): You’re right, so after confirming I probably won’t be the chief engineer of a major project, my attitude toward every course has been “wtf is this class even useful for”
  • Wuxing (2025-11-30 01:00): Still got real skills though
  • LoulanRE reply @Wuxing (2025-11-30 01:15): But dialectically and optimistically speaking, maybe this jack-of-all-trades generalist type could actually be quite fitting in the future — grasp the big picture and all aspects, let AI do the tedious and heavy lifting as the workhorse
  • Wuxing reply @LoulanRE (2025-11-30 01:16): Yeah exactly bro, interdisciplinary talent is invincible
  • Saya (2025-12-01 16:27): Seeing everyone else also studying Signals B makes me feel better

[^1]: First-level discipline — the top-level classification in China’s higher education discipline system. A student’s “first-level discipline”determines their official academic track, but cross-disciplinary curricula can place them in departments that don’t align with their primary classification.
[^2]: Curriculum/training program — the structured course plan prescribed by Chinese universities for each major, which can sometimes result in unusual combinations of required courses across departmental boundaries.

entry.title: Astronomy Club
entry.content:
Skipped evening self-study[^selfstudy] to go photograph the moon at the astronomy club

Because of relative motion, the moon that looks so still

Actually needs the telescope recalibrated every five or six minutes

Reminds me of the law of quantitative to qualitative change

Listening to the senior talk about the recent comet, how Saturn’s rings are facing us right now, things like that

Actually, when I first got into sci-fi novels, when I was playing Stellaris, I did a bit of astronomical research, anyway, whether academic or hobbyist, it’s all super demanding for an individual

Recalling this, I feel like the university astronomy club might be the last time I ever get to engage with anything astronomy-related

Makes me even more glad I came. From tomorrow I need to push further, try skipping classes[^skipping], don’t waste time on meaninglessness!

** Comments**

  • optional (2024-10-17 22:26): So beautiful
  • Ji (2024-10-17 22:47): Our astronomy club doesn’t need an interview, you just join directly, but it’s really active
  • funoru (2024-10-17 23:34): Astronomy club
  • Chen (2024-10-18 06:33): A freedom I envy

[^selfstudy]: Evening self-study ( is a mandatory supervised study period common in Chinese universities and high schools, typically running from 7, 0 PM. Attendance may be tracked.
[^skipping]: Skipping class is a small act of rebellion in Chinese universities, where attendance is often strictly monitored and can affect your grade, making it far riskier than in Western universities.

entry.title: 'Shanghai Business Trip: Watching the Match'
entry.content:
My Company Employs Me[^1]

Happened to have travel subsidies for spectating, so I dropped by Shanghai for fun

Learned so much at IQC[^2] yesterday

Met so many people from the research group, Yu-Gi-Oh / MG / Class Rep / Teacher Mao

The two grandmasters who’d already gotten into US master’s programs, everything they said was pure substance[^3]

Who knew Teacher Mao had eight years of tutoring experience

The way he tells stories and lays down life lessons instantly reminded me of He

Took photos with the mentors

K-sensei still remembered my voice

I’d booked two slots with him before

Social anxiety[^4] made it impossible to socialize at all, and I was so exhausted

Literally drained to the bone, went back and slept three hours before we even woke up

Hope next year I can make master level with more confidence

And arrive in style

That year, nineteen, IQC gold level, standing there like a nobody~[^5]

Back then, I swore through my tears, you will all see me~

[^1]: A pun on Descartes’ “Cogito ergo sum”(I think, therefore I am). In Chinese, “”becomes “???, replacing “think”( with “company”(, turning it into “My company employs me, therefore I am.”

[^2]: International Quant Championship, a quantitative finance competition hosted by WorldQuant.

[^3]: Literally “dry goods” in Chinese internet slang, meaning content with real substance and no fluff.

[^4]: (sh kng), literally “social phobia.”In Chinese internet usage, it’s a catch-all term ranging from clinical social anxiety to simply being introverted or socially awkward.

[^5]: Adapted from Eason Chan’s song, Exaggerated): “ｅ,, “That year, eighteen, school dance, standing there like a nobody.”The next line “』, (“you must see me”) continues the same song’s spirit of desperate longing to be noticed.

entry.title: MyGO Collab Store Blitz
entry.content:
Went to the Lawson[^1] MyGO!!!!! x Ave Mujica collab store[^2]
I figured the event spots would’ve been gone by this point
Just planning to drop by and take a look
When the clerk said there were still spots left, several people around me all went “Wait, there are still spots?!”
The synchronization rate was hilariously good
Right away someone brought up the news about the Shanghai collab store shutting down over queuing and scalping[^3] disputes, and the fridge magnet point-collection[^4] event being completely cleared out on day one
It’s exactly in places like this, the vibe of being surrounded by fellow fans is so fun
I already knew all of this. I even forgot about the point collection until the first night, checked the official site and saw only 100-something left out of 4000, dashed out the door and blitzed to the Lawson at the school gate. I was so happy when I got it
Feel like my brain juice is about to burst out, so satisfying aaaaaah









** Comments**

  • optional (2024-12-22 13:02): The area around the school gate is so bustling, so many neon ads
  • LoulanRE reply @optional (2024-12-22 13:14): No no, this is the scenery near the collab store. If only the area around school were actually this bustling

[^1]: Lawson, a Japanese convenience store chain with a massive presence in China, frequently hosting anime collaboration events.
[^2]: Collab store, short for “collaboration store,”a common concept in Japanese otaku culture where a retail location is temporarily themed around an anime or game franchise, offering exclusive merchandise and themed food.
[^3]: Scalping, the practice of buying limited merchandise in bulk to resell at inflated prices, a persistent problem at anime collaboration events in China.
[^4]: Point-collection campaign, a common promotional activity in Japan and China where purchasing specific items earns points that can be redeemed for exclusive merchandise.

entry.title: Ave Mujica (Pseudo) Finale Thoughts
entry.content:
So many feelings, the plot was a mess, but those last two episodes were so damn good
Lingering on, echoing with regret
[MyGO Mujica fan theories and wild speculation[^1], it’s rare to find a work this fiercely discussed
Just a shame, “never again did I taste beans as good as that night, never again did I see a show as fine as that one”[^2]]
Just as I was having these feelings, about to make peace with it all, There’s actually a sequel!!! I screamed the instant I found out
Gasping in sheer excitement, hands pressed against my chest, just wanting to calm down and thank the heavens





[^1]: “Fake medicine” is Chinese ACG fandom slang for fan-made theories and speculation that turn out to be false, essentially “wild fan theories”or “copium.”
[^2]: A famous quote from Lu Xun’s essay “Village Opera”, expressing nostalgic longing for a fleeting, irreplaceable experience.

entry.content:
Never again, follow the world as it sinks

At eighteen, you tell yourself

“Pathfinder”unfulfilled (We Three)[^1]

entry.title: 'Hearts of Iron IV: No Step Back'
entry.content:
The new Hearts of Iron IV DLC No Step Back dropped, and while I was firing up a Soviet playthrough to check out the new focus tree and decisions, I suddenly noticed the text under the difficulty selection — those few passages, resonant and full of power, paired with the New Year, really did feel like “a new year, a new beginning.”It’s exactly the moment to set out again, traveling light. Perhaps we all need a little momentum to carry us forward sometimes, to face the tense and exhausting days of our lives.

I looked it up online — starting from the second difficulty level, the quotes below are respectively from Eisenhower’s speech before the Normandy landings, Roosevelt’s address before D-Day, Patton’s pre-battle rally speech, and Churchill’s speech upon taking office in the House of Commons.

I have nothing to offer but blood, toil, tears, and sweat. — Winston Churchill

Consider this a New Year’s blessing — may you all enjoy triumphant fortune in your finals and in the new year, and march onward toward your destination!

entry.title: First Day of Shanghai Business Trip
entry.content:
Northeastouthwest Victorious Reunion[^1]

Having a meal at Saizeriya[^2] and fiercely fulfilling the dreams of a light novel[^3] fan

An ancient battlefield brimming with countless stories

Spiritual and physical satisfaction in equal measure









[^1]: “Victorious Reunion”, a term from Chinese revolutionary history referring to the merging of different Red Army units after long marches. Used here humorously to describe friends from northeast and southwest China finally meeting up.
[^2]: Saizeriya (ㄨ?, a Japanese chain of Italian-style family restaurants, legendary in China for its extremely low prices. It’s the go-to spot for students and young adults, essentially the meme-tier budget dining institution of Chinese college life.
[^3]: Light novel (, a Japanese publishing format targeting young adults, typically featuring anime-style illustrations and serialized storytelling. Many popular anime are adapted from light novels.

entry.title: Received a Beloved Ocean Painting
entry.content:
Prompted by receiving a painting of the sea that I really loved, I went to see the sea this week

I tried taking some photos. I originally thought I’d win with a sunrise-at-dawn kind of shot, but I slept too late, slept too deeply, completely missed the alarm (though I still woke up at 6:30 out of sheer biological clock inertia, just shows how terrifying school can be)

The results of my casual snaps were actually decent, Probably beginner’s luck, but since I was a little proud of them, I couldn’t resist showing off

I had imagined myself in a misty morning, entering some sort of transcendent, enlightening state of oneness with nature, waiting for the sun to rise, letting out affected melancholic sighs, things like “Walk on, the shore won’t keep our footprints for long”; “The sea sets sail from its own homeland, and I wake from my dreams”

Reality was: too little sleep. Although I realized I almost took the subway in the wrong direction, when heading to the seaside I ended up walking along the coast in the wrong direction. After clambering across a huge stretch of jagged, black, hard rocks, I finally found a nearly deserted beach (“nearly”because there was an old gentleman[^1] swimming)

I waded across the sand, tasted a mouthful of seawater, and had barely taken a few steps when I saw a huge dead squid

I beat a lightning-fast retreat back to shore, barely managed to finish listening to the playlist I’d carefully curated for my imagined aesthetic, then sighed and went to wash the sand off my feet at the tap

Maybe that’s why there were so few people here. At that thought, the old man finished his swim and started matter-of-factly showering under the tap next to me

He’s probably retired with time on his hands. I’m here because I happened to have a day off. So the lack of people today might also have something to do with the May Day shift adjustments[^2]. Thinking along these lines, I suddenly felt a sense of kindred spirits, “What night lacks moonlight, What place lacks bamboo and cypress, But few are the idlers like the two of us.”[^3]

I just wonder, did he see that dead squid, It was really conspicuous

My original takeaway was that for the first time, I was viscerally worried about ecological issues

But thinking about it now, I did see a fishing harbor along the way. A dead squid this large showing up near the shore, they must have been the ones who knocked it off, whether intentionally or not. Watch yourselves next time, will you?

** Comments**

  • Mangcai Pai Gaoceng (2024-04-21 22:54): Not gonna take a photo of the squid?

Footnotes

[^1]: “Old gentleman”(х), A casual, affectionate term of address for older men in everyday Chinese, roughly equivalent to “uncle”or “older gentleman.”It carries warmth and informality.
[^2]: May Day shift adjustments, China’s holiday scheduling system where makeup workdays are arranged around public holidays (such as the May 1st Labor Day) to create longer continuous breaks. This means people often must work on adjacent weekends to compensate, which can affect overall attendance and activity levels during the holiday period.
[^3]: “What night lacks moonlight, What place lacks bamboo and cypress, But few are the idlers like the two of us.”(, From the Song dynasty poet Su Shi’s Record of a Night Stroll at Chengtian Temple (╁). Written during Su Shi’s exile, this brief essay captures a spontaneous midnight walk with a friend under the moonlight, finding beauty and solace in shared leisure amid adversity.

entry.title: Found Out Where My Best Elementary School Friend Went
entry.content:
Found out where my best friend from elementary school ended up

He went back to his hometown for middle school. Starting from seventh grade we’d contact each other every summer vacation, but he’s also the only elementary school classmate I still keep in touch with

Even though I knew he chose the humanities track[^1] and had barely any free time, I was still shocked he got into Renmin University[^2], couldn’t help but feel deeply moved

And the moment I scrolled through his Qzone[^3], all the memories just came flooding back

Going to his house to play games together, after school walking out the gate to pick up his little sister, then for fun taking that path covered in shrubs barely two feet wide to walk home together, occasionally getting messed with by these bratty twin kids from our class on the way

Joining a recitation competition together and finding out for the first time he gets hypoglycemia

Borrowing a classmate’s Hitler: A Biography together and being profoundly shaken, drawing lots of little booklets about politics and history, so captivated by the text that I couldn’t stop wanting to say so much that my sections ended up being all dialogue

Being close with my desk-mate, getting accused by the homeroom teacher[^4] of “early love”[^5], I forget why he was there too, but anyway the three of us were squatting in the hallway during lunch break while the teacher smiled and talked to us

Later he told me when my desk-mate transferred here in fourth grade, she smiled so radiantly and brilliantly, he’d liked her since then, he said, his eyes full of wistful reminiscence

But honestly I didn’t care at all, I just thought it was fun hanging out with her. I even felt like it cut into time with my other good friends. Looking back now, it seems like she really did get jealous when I chatted with other girls

Right here, I suddenly remembered, I was so confident and outgoing in elementary school. Not just that, so many things have changed, and there are parts of myself I really hate now. What on earth happened?

I don’t know how this line just popped into my head

“Hey, what kind of person do you want to be when you grow up?”

“I’ll answer with the rest of my life!”

** Comments**

  • Chen (2024-11-10 09:18): Dan-ge, you’ll become a good adult
  • LoulanRE reply @Chen (2024-11-10 13:25): Gotta work hard to become a splendid adult, all of us
  • Oblivionis (2024-11-10 10:02): Already added to the winter break creative list
  • LoulanRE reply @Oblivionis (2024-11-10 13:26):, Not sure I follow
  • optional (2024-11-10 10:08): Changes are also caused by experiences, no need to be hard on yourself
  • LoulanRE reply @optional (2024-11-10 13:45): It’s just self-dissatisfaction. With an immature mind, I could only be passively shaped by experiences back then, but now I intend to actively change for the better
  • optional reply @LoulanRE (2024-11-10 14:22): Yeah, I support you!!
  • abc reply @LoulanRE (2024-11-10 18:05): Becoming more open and confident, that’s seriously impressive, total domination
  • ?. (2024-11-10 11:49): The twin brats
  • LoulanRE reply @?. (2024-11-10 13:34): As absurd as it sounds, it’s the truth. Looking back at elementary school, I feel half proud and half nostalgic, so vivid and distant, as if it were someone else’s life
  • ?. reply @LoulanRE (2024-11-10 13:35): Agreed, +1
  • Oblivionis (2024-11-10 13:58): Winter break filming script reference
  • Ji reply @Oblivionis (2024-11-10 19:31): I want to see it

Footnotes

[^1]: “Humanities track”, In China’s high school streaming system, students are divided into a sciences track and a humanities/liberal arts track, typically around 10th grade.
[^2]: Renmin University (ぇ), Renmin University of China, one of China’s most prestigious universities, particularly renowned for humanities and social sciences.
[^3]: Qzone, QQ Zone, a social networking platform within Tencent’s QQ ecosystem, widely used in China for blogging and sharing updates, especially popular among the millennial generation.
[^4]: Homeroom teacher (, In Chinese schools, the homeroom teacher holds far more authority than in Western schools, responsible for discipline, moral education, and overall student welfare, essentially acting as the primary authority figure for a class.
[^5]: “Early love”(╂), Literally “early love,”a pejorative term used in Chinese education to refer to romantic relationships among middle and high school students, which are generally discouraged or prohibited by schools and parents.

entry.title: Quant Finance: Zero to Hero
entry.content:
TL;DR: MAN WANTED — Quantitative Finance — Join a research group of my acquaintances — Solid income

Officially speaking, I’m currently developing quantitative strategies for WorldQuant as an Independent Consultant

How did a CS major end up in quantitative finance?
Because the pay is just too damn good

You might think — oh, it’s an ad, not interested
But I genuinely think this is a great fit
I hope you’ll think seriously after reading what follows

You should not throw away your shot

As long as you’re interested, you can join and see what we do every day, how much we earn
The more you know, the better choice you’ll make — I believe that

I started from zero too — if you want to do it, you can

Benefits of joining:
Learn Python, guided paper reading sessions, how to go from reading to reproducing papers
Monthly global online briefings, biweekly 15-minute slots with mentors (Central University of Finance and Economics undergrad, fully-funded Cambridge master’s)

Most importantly, the new PPAC rules — even if you have zero ideas and zero willingness to learn, just mindlessly running code for the basic stipend gets you 5k RMB pre-tax per year
Before, I’d have said this job has great potential — the highest daily base payment can reach 120 USD — but now: stop talking and come fleece the Americans[^1]
Of course, if you’re willing to improve quantity and quality, the income goes way beyond that

Saw a fellow college student on the forum who joined in October 2024 — their Q1 2025 quarterly bonus alone was at least 8k USD
So envious, and secretly thinking maybe I could do that too

Yes, this company WorldQuant operates on exactly this strategy — unique in the industry
Three cobblers with their wits combined equal Zhuge Liang the master strategist[^2]

Their database is enormous — hiring full-salary US-based quant analysts is too expensive, and one person’s energy is limited
So they lower the barrier to entry, lower the salary, and do it remotely worldwide
But thanks to the exchange rate difference, a salary US quant analysts would scoff at is serious money for a Chinese college student like me

In reality, most of the time is spent learning and thinking of ideas — actual daily work time is probably no more than an hour
It’s remote and online — want income, Submit alphas. Don’t want to, Zero pressure

Knowledge monetization is insanely fast — newly learned knowledge and fresh ideas immediately go into producing more alphas
I have to admit I stepped in tons of pitfalls — cloud servers, Linux, Python, HTTP, finance knowledge, all sorts of issues
But every day you submit an alpha, you get income — solve problems → earn income

Hard work actually pays off — in this era of involution[^3] and confusion, having this kind of positive feedback loop is such a precious treasure

Sir, I want to progress so badly[^4]

I’ve entered a different state of living — cheerful and diligent
Because hard work paid off, and it paid a lot and paid fast
At least I think the work I do is absolutely not worth that much money

Every day when the US Eastern Daylight Time rolls over to a new day, I can see how much I earned from submitted alphas
It’s addictive. Seriously addictive
Now I spend extra time each day on daydreaming

Sincerely inviting you to join our research group
Don’t be shy — it’s not some big organization, just people I know
We already have two signed consultants, the rest have also submitted their initial reviews

Because of non-compete agreements, there’s almost no one here with serious finance expertise
Everything I know I learned from scratch — and on the platform it’s even more so, inexplicably lots of old fogeys[^5] who probably faint at the sight of code
But things here are still not simple — you know in China, high returns + not difficult + few people is an impossible triangle[^6]
But the platform has only been live for two years, only 8k consultants globally, only 400 active in the China region — I feel lucky to even know about this

This matters to me too — last quarter I missed out on 30 USD in income because I didn’t know and lacked help
Studying alone without friends leads to narrow-mindedness and ignorance[^7]

I’ve already stepped through all the pitfalls, cleared all the obstacles, and can smooth out the learning curve to help everyone through the tough spots
Don’t worry — when a referral succeeds, the platform pays me a referral fee — consider it my tuition for teaching and providing on-demand tutoring

As long as you’re interested, you can join and see what we do every day, how much we earn
The more you know, the better choice you’ll make — I believe that

Tomorrow the China region holds its June info session
June 9th is the global consultants’ briefing (what a date to reflect on)
You can join as an observer, find me on the voice channel for Q&A!

Based on this earn-rice[^8] group, we want to form a close-knit study group with real connections
Welcome aboard
We need more friends and mutual support

💬 Comments

  • Dawn (2025-05-29 01:13): Yi was right — it was Yi who recommended this at first, I started during winter break, but suddenly encountering an unfamiliar field, and being far from actually earning the basic stipend, having real income, took a long time, so motivation was low. It was still Yi who stepped through many pitfalls upfront and slowly guided me onto the right track. I also successfully became a consultant and started collecting the basic stipend. Though seeing how much GM level earners make, I still get envious, so maybe that’s the drive to level up. If you also want to earn the basic stipend, like Yi said, you really can know nothing, just run the templates, wait for approval, and submit under PPAC rules — you can easily earn the basic stipend too. If you want 5000 a year in basic income, give it a try
  • LoulanRE (2025-05-29 07:20): In reality the study time is only about 20h, it’s just the initial and secondary review process is too long, but since I’ve already hit the pitfalls, I have ways to greatly reduce the queue time in the pool
  • Mangcai Pai Gaoceng (2025-05-29 08:05): Bro this is sounding more and more like a pyramid scheme
  • LoulanRE reply @Mangcai Pai Gaoceng (2025-05-29 08:11): The platform and income are really so mysterious, it genuinely is starting to sound like a pyramid scheme
  • LoulanRE reply @Mangcai Pai Gaoceng (2025-05-29 08:13): It’s all because of PPAC, even the basic stipend bros get their spring
  • LoulanRE reply @Mangcai Pai Gaoceng (2025-05-29 08:19): Now this is true gratitude — no merit, but at least there’s suffering
  • Mangcai Pai Gaoceng reply @LoulanRE (2025-05-29 08:28): It’s fine, I did it too
  • cloud (2025-05-29 17:19): I’m back to collect the basic stipend
  • LoulanRE reply @cloud (2025-05-29 17:37): Mainly it’s PPAC, as long as Sharpe > 1, they don’t check other performance or correlation, it’s basically giving money away
  • LoulanRE reply @cloud (2025-05-29 17:37): Come

[^1]: — literally “plucking wool off the Americans,”a Chinese slang expression for profiting off the exchange rate differential between USD and RMB. What counts as modest pay in the US becomes substantial income in China.

[^2]: — a Chinese proverb meaning “Three humble cobblers with their wits combined equal Zhuge Liang the master strategist.”Zhuge Liang is the legendary genius strategist from the Three Kingdoms era (181–234 AD). The proverb expresses that collective wisdom can rival even the greatest individual mind.

[^3]: (nèi juǎn) — literally “involution.”Originally an anthropological term, it has become Chinese internet slang for hyper-competition where everyone works harder but nobody gets ahead — a zero-sum rat race with diminishing returns.

[^4]:, — “Teacher, I want to progress so badly.”A famous quote from the Chinese political drama <> (In the Name of the People, 2017), spoken by the character Qi Tongwei. It’s used ironically online to express an almost desperate ambition to climb the ladder.

[^5]: (lǎo dēng) — Chinese internet slang, a slightly disrespectful term for an old person, roughly equivalent to “old fogey”or “boomer.”Often used humorously rather than maliciously.

[^6]: — the “impossible trinity”or “impossible triangle.”An economics concept (also known as the Mundell-Fleming trilemma) stating that three desirable properties cannot all be achieved simultaneously. The author humorously applies it to: high returns, low difficulty, and few competitors being mutually exclusive in China.

[^7]:, — from <·> (Book of Rites, chapter “Xueji / Record of Education”), circa 1st century BC. Full meaning: “Studying alone without friends leads to narrow-mindedness and scant knowledge.”One of the earliest Chinese texts arguing for collaborative learning.

[^8]: (zhuàn mǐ) — literally “earn rice.”Chinese internet slang for “”(make money), using the homophone (mǐ, rice) for (qián, money). A playful way to say “earning a living.”

entry.title: December 25 1991 - The Soviet Flag Slowly Descended Over the Kremlin
entry.content:
On December 25, 1991, at 19:32, the Soviet flag slowly descended over the Kremlin, replaced by the Slavic tricolor.

“The entire Red Square lay pitch-black, like an unfathomable ocean — only the howling freezing wind and that colossal red star atop the Spasskaya Tower watched over this night with cold indifference.”

Gazing back into endless silence amid the cold, there was once a time when the fire of ideals ignited this eternally bitter land. The deep-winter wind sends one into a daze — as if the October Revolution, the Five-Year Plans, the Battle of Stalingrad, the voyages into space, were all but fleeting bubbles.

Liberating all humanity versus the war in Afghanistan; fighting for the emancipation of the proletariat versus the “Party of the Entire People”; democratic progress versus the Prague Spring; the man who set sail from Riga — Sablin — branded a counter-revolutionary; the radiance of Red October fading away; a great ideal vulgarly reworked time and again by hollow slogans, the spark of yestear extinguished by the long night, becoming a shrine plaque for someone else’s promotion, profit, and privilege.

Faith shattered, reality barren — was the agony compressed over so long to be released, or to endure, Yet on the day it truly fell, the heart had already gone numb past all response. You couldn’t call it pain or joy, nor regret or relief. An era ended, leaving only the silence that forever stands in the freezing wind.

Who stands behind the flag now on that Red Square where birch leaves once fell, The prosperity Yeltsin promised — thirty years have passed. Who knows when history will turn a new page.

It was not Qin that destroyed the six states — the six states destroyed themselves. It was not the world that destroyed Qin — Qin destroyed itself. If those who come after mourn them but do not learn from them, they too will be mourned by those who come after them. — Du Mu, “Epitaph for the Palace of Ah”

May Aurora — forever the goddess of the dawn — guide us toward the light!

💬 Comments

  • Qingwan (2021-12-25 20:17): Once, my foreign language was Russian

entry.title: Foggy Chongqing
entry.content:
This here is the fog city Chongqing[^1]

Autumn and winter, it’s either rain or fog

Love my average of 104 foggy days per year?

Because of rain, the physical fitness test[^2] got postponed four weeks in a row

An entire month with barely a glimpse of sunlight

A kid who grew up in Xiamen just can’t adapt

Sunlight drops, serotonin levels plummet

Daily relapses of unexplained low mood

Simply enveloped by the bleak, cold melancholy of winter

Quick, summon Master Hina[^3]

Desperately need a 100% Sunshine Girl[^3]

Ample sunlight is an absolutely vital necessity of life

** Comments**

  • Sanjiaomao (2024-12-06 21:55): From your very first sentence, it seems you’ve already started blending into this city hahahahahahaha
  • LoulanRE reply @Sanjiaomao (2024-12-06 22:01): Picked it up from the locals, but they really don’t like speaking Mandarin, feels a bit uncomfortable
  • Sanjiaomao reply @LoulanRE (2024-12-06 22:04): If you can’t beat them, join them. Suggest you thoroughly study Sichuanese, the universally understood dialect nationwide
  • Sanjiaomao (2024-12-06 21:56): Also why Hina?
  • LoulanRE reply @Sanjiaomao (2024-12-06 22:00):, I’m culturally illiterate[^4]
  • Sanjiaomao (2024-12-06 21:59): Come visit the Rain City next time to balance things out (it’s raining here right now too)
  • LoulanRE reply @Sanjiaomao (2024-12-06 22:12): Sigh, the Sichuan Basin
  • optional (2024-12-06 22:23): At least it’s humid, right?
  • Sanjiaomao reply @optional (2024-12-06 22:30): I want to go somewhere dry and cold
  • LoulanRE reply @optional (2024-12-06 22:36): I want to pack up all the moisture and ship it away
  • optional reply @Sanjiaomao (2024-12-06 23:25): In reality the wind makes it impossible to take a single step
  • Mangcai Pai Gaoceng (2024-12-06 23:13): Hina
  • LoulanRE reply @Mangcai Pai Gaoceng (2024-12-06 23:23): Another daily cultural illiteracy[^4] moment, my bad

Footnotes

[^1]: “This here is the fog city Chongqing”(, Written in Chongqing dialect. “, (l) is the local dialect pronunciation of “, (zh), meaning “this.”The phrase mimics a well-known local expression.
[^2]: Physical fitness test, A mandatory physical fitness assessment required of all university students in China, covering events like running, long jump, and sit-ups. Failing may affect graduation.
[^3]: Hina / Sunshine Girl (/コ), Refers to Hina Amano (╅), the heroine of Makoto Shinkai’s 2019 anime film Weathering with You (╂). She possesses the supernatural ability to clear the sky by praying, making her a “sunshine girl”(コ). The author initially misremembered her name as “”(Haru) instead of the correct “”(Hina), which the commenters teasingly correct.
[^4]: “Culturally illiterate”, Internet slang, a humorous homophonic variant of (wn mng, illiterate/uncultured), used self-deprecatingly when one fails to recognize a well-known cultural reference.

entry.title: TLJH Lightweight JupyterHub Deployment Guide: One Server for Your Team's Data Analysis Environment
entry.content:
TLJH Lightweight JupyterHub Deployment Guide

Table of Contents

  1. What is TLJH and Why Choose It
  2. Connecting to the Server
  3. Deploying TLJH
  4. Global Python Environment Configuration
  5. Appendix: One-Click Download Install Script

What is TLJH and Why Choose It

1.1 One Server = The Entire Team’s Development Environment

The Littlest JupyterHub (abbreviated TLJH) is the official lightweight multi-user computing platform from the Jupyter project. Its core selling point is dead simple:

Use a single ordinary Linux server to provide the entire team with a browser-ready Jupyter environment.

Once deployed, all you need to do is share the server IP with your colleagues. They open a browser, enter their credentials, and immediately get an independent Jupyter workspace, with pandas, scikit-learn, PyTorch, and other libraries you pre-installed ready to go out of the box.

1.2 The Best Solution When Resources Are Tight: TLJH vs Vanilla JupyterHub

JupyterHub itself is already a mature multi-user Jupyter platform, but it’s designed by default for medium-to-large organizations: it requires a separate database, a scalable proxy layer, and complex authentication configuration. TLJH (The Littlest JupyterHub) is the official streamlined distribution designed for the single-server scenario, and the core difference lies in its trade-off design when resources are tight.

Comparison TLJH Vanilla JupyterHub (Manual Deployment)
Number of servers 1 is enough At least 2 (Hub + separate proxy/database)
Minimum spec 2-core 4GB cloud server 4-core 8GB minimum, separate database adds overhead
Deployment method One command, 5 minutes Manual configuration of Hub, Proxy, database, Systemd
User isolation System-level users + shared Conda environment Container/Docker isolation, independent image per user
Resource usage Only 128MB base overhead Single user container takes hundreds of MB, Hub itself extra
Scale limit 1, 00 users Unlimited, but each additional node adds cost

The key trade-off is the level of user isolation:

  • Vanilla JupyterHub recommends Docker isolation by default, with each user in an independent container. This is indeed more secure and more “cloud-native,”but on a 2-core 4GB server, spinning up 5 user instances can consume all available memory.
  • TLJH’s choice is system-level users + shared Python environment. All users run on the same operating system, sharing the Conda environment under /opt/tljh/user. No container overhead, no image layering, 128MB of base memory sustains the Hub itself, and the remaining resources are entirely available for users’ actual computation.

Bottom line: If you only have one server and a team of fewer than 100 people, TLJH is the only solution that can actually run under resource constraints. It sacrifices container-level strong isolation in exchange for the ability to serve more users on limited hardware, for a trusted internal team environment, this is a perfectly reasonable trade-off.

1.3 TLJH Architecture Overview

1.4 These Scenarios Are a Perfect Fit for TLJH

  • Teaching/Training: Instructor pre-installs the course environment, students log in and start using it, say goodbye to “environment setup as the first lesson”
  • Data analysis teams: Unified Python versions and dependencies, eliminating “it works on my machine”once and for all
  • Lightweight GPU sharing: Single-GPU server with multi-user time-sharing, more VRAM-efficient than Docker solutions
  • Remote development environment: Replace local Anaconda, access from any browser anywhere, seamless migration across machines

Connecting to the Server

This guide assumes you’re using a cloud server (Tencent Cloud, Alibaba Cloud, Huawei Cloud, etc.).

  1. Get server information: Public IP, admin account (usually root), password
  2. Open a terminal:
  • Windows: Press Win + R, type cmd, or open PowerShell
  • macOS/Linux: Open Terminal
  1. Establish SSH connection: ssh root@
  2. Authentication:
  • On first connection, if you see a security confirmation prompt, type yes and press Enter
  • Enter your password (Linux doesn’t echo password input, just type it and press Enter)

Deploying TLJH

3.1 System Requirements

  • OS: Ubuntu 22.04+ or Debian 11+
  • Architecture: amd64 or arm64
  • Minimum spec: 2 CPU cores / 4GB RAM / 20GB disk
  • Recommended spec: 4 CPU cores / 8GB RAM / 50GB disk (team of up to 10)

3.2 The Core Question: Slow Installation on China Cloud Servers, What to Do?

This is the most critical part of this guide.

TLJH’s official install script needs to pull code from GitHub and install dependencies from PyPI. But cloud servers in China have extremely slow access to GitHub and the official PyPI, sometimes timing out entirely. You don’t need to configure a proxy for the entire server, just specify mirror sources temporarily during installation.

Solution: Use environment variables to inject mirror URLs

TLJH’s install script supports configuring mirrors via environment variables, with no need to modify the script itself. Run the following command (replace admin at the end with your desired admin username):

How it works:

  • TLJH_GIT_MIRROR: Proxies GitHub raw content and git repository requests. ghproxy.net is a commonly used GitHub acceleration service in China; you can also substitute https://mirror.ghproxy.com/ or other trusted proxies
  • TLJH_PYPI_MIRROR: Proxies Python package downloads. pypi.tuna.tsinghua.edu.cn is the Tsinghua University TUNA mirror, stable and well-synced

If your server is overseas, or you already have another proxy setup, just run the official command directly, no environment variables needed:

3.3 Deployment Flowchart

3.4 Wait for Installation to Complete

The installation process typically takes 5, 5 minutes, and the terminal will continuously output logs. When you see Done! or return to the command prompt, deployment is complete.

During installation, TLJH automatically handles the following:

  • Installs Python 3, pip, git, and other base dependencies
  • Creates isolated Hub and user environments
  • Configures systemd services for auto-start on boot
  • Sets up Traefik reverse proxy, automatically handling HTTP/HTTPS

Global Python Environment Configuration

4.1 Why Global Configuration Is Needed

TLJH comes with a built-in Miniforge-based Conda environment under /opt/tljh/user, no need to install Anaconda/Miniforge yourself. All regular users share the Python libraries in this environment by default.

This means: the admin installs once, and everyone can use it.

4.2 Environment Configuration Flow

4.3 Log In to the Web Interface to Verify

Enter the server’s public IP in your browser’s address bar to access the JupyterHub login page:

  • Username: the admin username set during installation
  • Password: the password you enter the first time will be automatically set as your permanent password

4.3 Activate the Global Environment and Configure Mirror Sources

After connecting to the server via SSH, the system doesn’t load TLJH’s shared environment by default. Before installing libraries, you must manually activate it first:

After successful activation, (base) or a similar indicator will appear in your command prompt.

Configure Conda mirror sources (using Tsinghua University mirrors as an example):

Note: sudo -E is required, it ensures that the currently activated Conda environment variables are inherited under root privileges.

Configure Pip mirror source:

If you don’t need China mirrors, you can skip the above configuration and use the default sources directly.

4.4 Install Python Libraries for All Users

With the environment still activated, use the following commands to install the libraries your team needs:

Install with Conda (recommended, fewer dependency conflicts):

Install with Pip:

After installation, all regular users on JupyterHub can directly import these libraries without individual configuration.

Appendix: One-Click Download Install Script

Below is a general-purpose bootstrap script I modified based on the official one. The key improvement is support for flexibly configuring mirror sources via environment variables, without modifying the script itself.

Usage:

Script source code (click the button above to download directly):
tljh-bootstrap.py download file

References

  • TLJH Official Documentation
  • TLJH GitHub Repository
  • JupyterHub Official Documentation
